took: The catalog of generatable CLIs (45+ APIs); the dual CLI+MCP output model; the 35-100x token-efficiency claim (CLIs pre-format output, MCP servers dump raw JSON) that motivates wrapping CLIs as the execution engine while keeping MCP as the agent-facing interface; the default endpoint-mirror mode that produces ~30-50 tools per API and motivates the aggregation/facade design.

Per-tool auth-isolation pattern (each sandboxed binary gets only its own credentials, never the full env); the 'one-command auth' UX shape; the idea of treating credential scoping as a first-class concern of the sandbox boundary rather than a deployment afterthought. Applied at per-call granularity in cocoon instead of pillbox's per-session granularity.

The SandboxPolicy-over-OS-primitives abstraction: one declarative policy translated to bwrap+Landlock on Linux, Seatbelt on macOS, Restricted Tokens on Windows. The pattern of namespace isolation flags (--unshare-user, --unshare-pid, --unshare-net) and their tradeoffs. The arg0-based subprocess invocation pattern (which we noted but did not adopt — calling bwrap/Seatbelt directly avoids the Codex dependency).

Layered permissions model (permission mode + sandbox + per-tool allow-list + hooks) as the right shape for an agent-facing sandbox. The proxy-based network egress allowlist via unix domain socket (queued for cocoon v1.1) — bwrap's --unshare-net is all-or-nothing, so per-host control requires an out-of-sandbox proxy. The observation that sandboxing reduces permission prompts ~84% in internal use, which validates the 'tight default, no prompts' shape rather than 'open default + prompt on each action'.
